architecture.summary: The paper develops Process Reward Models (PRMs) that score intermediate reasoning steps in LLM outputs for mathematical reasoning tasks. The system reads step-by-step reasoning chains from an LLM and predicts correctness scores at the step level, enabling Best-of-N decoding to select high-quality solution paths. Information flows from LLM-generated reasoning traces into a reward model that outputs per-step correctness judgments, which are aggregated into path-level scores for ranking candidate solutions.

biblio.title: The Lessons of Developing Process Reward Models in Mathematical Reasoning

claims.statement: Commonly used Monte Carlo (MC) estimation-based data synthesis for PRMs typically yields inferior performance and generalization compared to LLM-as-a-judge and human annotation methods
Conventional Best-of-N (BoN) evaluation strategies for PRMs contain potential biases
A consensus filtering mechanism effectively integrates MC estimation with LLM-as-a-judge

A more comprehensive evaluation framework combining response-level and step-level metrics significantly improves both model performance and data efficiency in the BoN evaluation and the step-wise error identification task
A new state-of-the-art PRM outperforms existing open-source alternatives
